items.title: BTC firmly rejected at its 200-day MA despite a bipartisan Senate crypto bill clearing committee
items.idea: The US Senate Banking Committee advanced the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act to the full Senate in a bipartisan 15-9 vote a few weeks ago. Despite that regulatory progress, BTC was firmly rejected at its 200-day moving average and has traded down since, now at $63,918, -26.97% YTD, trading below both the 50- and 200-day SMAs.
items.angle: Divergence between the political tailwind and price action is the real signal here — it says the overhang is leverage/positioning, not regulatory uncertainty, and no bill fixes that.
items.tweetA: Bipartisan Senate bill advances crypto market structure 15-9. BTC's reaction: get rejected at the 200-day and keep bleeding, -27% YTD. When good regulatory news can't move price, the problem isn't the news.
items.tweetB: Everyone said 'regulatory clarity is the BTC catalyst.' We got it — Senate Banking passed the Clarity Act 15-9. BTC promptly failed its 200D MA. Maybe the catalyst was never regulation, it was leverage unwinding, and no bill fixes that. $BTC

charts.desc: BTC daily chart with 50/200 SMA and 21 EMA overlay, part of a larger multi-ticker technical update.

items.title: Gold loses its 200-day moving average for the first time since the 2023 bull run began
items.idea: Gold (-4.69% YTD) lost its 200-day moving average for the first time since the current bull run started in late 2023, and was rejected at the descending 21-day EMA twice this week. The $4,000 level is holding, but bulls need to reclaim the near-term moving averages; a pullback to $3,700-$3,900 is flagged as an area to add physical.
items.angle: Two-plus years of relentless uptrend, one broken level — the first 200-day loss of this cycle is a structural tell worth tracking regardless of near-term bounce attempts.
items.tweetA: Gold just lost its 200-day moving average for the first time since the bull run started in late 2023. Two years of uptrend, one broken level. $4,000 is holding — barely. The real test is $3,700-$3,900.
items.tweetB: Gold miners have been priced for $4,500 gold. Spot just broke its 200-day for the first time in the entire 2023-26 bull run. If sentiment resets before price does, the miners are the more interesting short than gold itself. $GLD $GDX

charts.desc: Gold daily chart showing rejection at the 21 EMA and loss of the 200 SMA.

items.title: MU +210% YTD and SNDK +596% YTD are riding the same AI memory trade — but just diverged technically
items.idea: MU is up 210.65% YTD and SNDK up 596.09% YTD, both riding the AI memory shortage trade. But MU just posted its first bearish 5/21 EMA cross since March 25 on high-volume selling and lost its 21-day EMA, while SNDK held its 50-day SMA and closed the week above every key moving average.
items.angle: Same macro story, diverging technical structure — the market is starting to price the memory trade as two different bets, not one basket.
items.tweetA: MU +210% YTD, SNDK +596% YTD — both riding the same AI memory shortage. But MU just printed its first bearish 5/21 EMA cross since March while SNDK reclaimed every moving average. The memory trade is no longer one trade.
items.tweetB: Nobody's asking why SNDK is up 6x and MU 'only' 3x this year on the same memory shortage story. Different NAND vs DRAM mix, different customer base. When a 600%-YTD stock diverges technically from its 200%-YTD 'twin,' that's the market pricing something specific, not thematic. $MU $SNDK

charts.desc: MU and SNDK daily charts with 21/50/200 MA overlays showing the bearish/bullish EMA cross divergence.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / BofA
items.title: BofA now sees three more Fed hikes this year (75bps) — not cuts — as the industrial cycle reaccelerates
items.idea: Bank of America's economists see room for three rate increases this year (75bps total), arguing activity is still firm, unemployment low, and inflation stubborn. Their proprietary Global Wave and Industrial Momentum Indicator are both improving, and the Truck Shipper Survey is running 13% above last year, pushing back toward record territory.
items.angle: While the Street debates how many cuts are left, BofA just called for the Fed to go the opposite direction entirely — that's the real tail risk nobody is pricing into vol or credit spreads.
items.tweetA: BofA: 3 more hikes this year, 75bps total. While the Street debates how many cuts we get, one shop just called for the Fed to go the other way entirely. Freight and Korean exports are both running hot — that's the tell nobody's watching.
items.tweetB: The consensus trade all year has been 'the only question is how many cuts.' BofA just said: wrong question. If they're right and hikes come back on the table with equities at highs, the drawdown isn't priced anywhere — not in vol, not in credit spreads.

charts.desc: BofA Global Wave / Industrial Momentum Indicator chart (paywalled beyond the preview).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / BofA
items.title: The AI trade is losing its monopoly on capital, per BofA — flows rotating to small caps, financials, healthcare
items.idea: BofA flags heavy hyperscaler capex, deteriorating free cash flow and fading momentum pushing investors toward small caps, financials, healthcare and other neglected sectors. With after-tax real cash yields near -1%, BofA sees household liquidity rotating into equities and credit broadly, not just AI-heavy benchmarks.
items.angle: This lines up with Eliant's read the same week: the Nasdaq's strength came from AI 'spenders' beating AI 'receivers,' a narrower rotation within AI itself — consistent with BofA's FCF-deterioration thesis, not a contradiction of it.
items.tweetA: BofA's chart: hyperscaler capex is eating free cash flow so fast that AI stocks are losing their monopoly on new money — small caps, financials, healthcare are picking up the flow. Same week, the Nasdaq's strength was AI 'spenders' beating AI 'receivers,' not AI beating everything. Same rotation, two sources.
items.tweetB: Real after-tax cash yields near -1%. That's the real story this week: BofA isn't saying 'sell AI,' they're saying cash is now expensive to hold, so the marginal dollar is finally leaving the sidelines for anything with relative value — including names that aren't Mag 7.

charts.desc: No chart in the free preview; full note is paywalled.

items.title: Rep. Ro Khanna says armed Israeli settlers with US-made M4 rifles held his West Bank convoy for 90 minutes
items.idea: Khanna says his van was surrounded near Khirbet Zanuta by Israeli settlers carrying US-made M4 rifles while IDF personnel mediated rather than cleared the road; his aide put the detention at over an hour, Khanna himself at 90 minutes. The US gives Israel $3.3B in annual Foreign Military Financing plus $500M in missile-defense support — if the rifles are confirmed US-origin, this becomes an end-use-control question, not just a travel-security story.
items.angle: The interesting part isn't the detention itself — it's the accountability channel. When Palestinians or NGOs get stopped at a settler roadblock it's a human-rights report; when it's a sitting US Congressman, it becomes a congressional oversight file with a decision point as soon as Monday, July 13.
items.tweetA: A sitting member of Congress says he was held for 90 minutes by settlers carrying US-made M4s while the IDF just mediated. If those rifles are confirmed US-origin, this becomes an end-use-control question for a $3.8B annual aid package, not a travel story.
items.tweetB: Same roadblock, different accountability channel. Palestinians and NGOs get stopped by armed settlers constantly and it stays a human-rights file. A US Congressman gets stopped once and it becomes a congressional oversight decision due Monday. That gap is the actual story.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Hartnett Flow Show
items.title: The 30-year real yield just hit its highest level since November 2008
items.idea: Hartnett's Flow Show opens with the real yield on the 30-year Treasury climbing to its highest level since November 2008 — the depths of the GFC — as his headline warning that financial conditions are tightening beneath a market sitting near highs.
items.angle: Real yields at a 17-year high while equities sit near record highs is exactly the kind of divergence that historically doesn't resolve quietly.
items.tweetA: 30-year real yield just hit its highest level since November 2008 — the depths of the GFC. Equities are near highs. One of these two charts is wrong, and it's probably not the bond market.
items.tweetB: Everyone's framing 2026 as 'when do cuts arrive.' The 30Y real yield says financial conditions are the tightest they've been in 17 years, cuts or not. That's not a rate-path story, that's a term-premium story, and nobody's hedging for it.

charts.desc: No chart in the free preview; full note is paywalled.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Hartnett Flow Show
items.title: Hartnett's once-mocked 25/25/25/25 portfolio is annualizing 16% this year, its best since 2021
items.idea: The equal-weight 25/25/25/25 mix (stocks, bonds, cash, commodities) that Wall Street dismissed as a gimmick alternative to 60/40 is now annualizing a 16% return YTD, its best showing since 2021 — evidence that in a regime where inflation, fiscal excess and policy uncertainty keep shifting under both stocks and bonds, diversification has become part of the return engine rather than a defensive afterthought.
items.angle: A dumb, mechanical, equal-weight-four-assets portfolio beating most active allocation only happens when stock-bond correlation has actually broken down — that's the real signal, not the 16% number itself.
items.tweetA: Hartnett's 'silly' 25/25/25/25 portfolio — equal parts stocks, bonds, cash, commodities — is annualizing 16% this year, its best since 2021. That number only happens when stocks and bonds stop being negatively correlated. The 60/40 playbook assumption is quietly broken.
items.tweetB: A dumb, mechanical, equal-weight-four-assets portfolio is beating most active allocation this year. That's not a case for indexing — it's a tell that nobody actually knows which regime we're in, so the market is rewarding whoever hedges instead of predicts.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Hartnett Flow Show
items.title: Hartnett: H2 consensus is too comfortable — no landing, no hike, no AI capex cut, no Dem sweep
items.idea: Hartnett's central warning is that the second-half consensus has priced out four distinct risks at once: no landing, no Fed hike, no AI capex cut, no Democratic sweep. As long as MAGS holds its 200-day moving average and AUD/JPY stays above 110, investors are more likely to rotate and reload than retreat from risk; below those levels, the calculus flips.
items.angle: This pairs directly with the same-day BofA note betting against one of Hartnett's four 'safe' assumptions outright — 3 more hikes. When two shops start disagreeing on what's supposedly priced out, volatility is being mispriced somewhere.
items.tweetA: Hartnett's naming four things the market assumes won't happen: no landing, no hike, no AI capex cut, no Dem sweep. BofA published a note the same week betting against one of those four directly — 3 more hikes. When two shops disagree on 'safe' assumptions, that's when vol gets mispriced.
items.tweetB: Hartnett's tripwires: MAGS above its 200-day, AUD/JPY above 110. Nobody's watching an FX cross to time a mega-cap rotation, but that's exactly the kind of relationship that snaps first when the market stops pricing 'no AI capex cut' as a given.

charts.desc: No chart in the free preview; full note is paywalled.

items.title: This week's Nasdaq strength was AI 'spenders' beating AI 'receivers,' not AI beating everything
items.idea: The Q's finished as the best-performing major index this week, helped by the recent strength of AI 'spenders' over 'receivers,' while small caps were the worst performer — though only lower by ~50bps. Since starting in June 2023, Eliant's strategy has returned +191.26% vs QQQ +106.38%, SPX +81.63%, Dow +63.87% and Russell +69.65%, at an 81.6% win rate (avg winner +30.52%, avg loser -15.40%).
items.angle: 'Spenders vs receivers' is a sharper lens than 'AI up or down' — it's about who captures the capex dollar versus who's writing the check, and it's the same rotation BofA is flagging from the FCF side.
items.tweetA: This week's AI trade wasn't AI up or down — it was 'spenders' (capex beneficiaries) outperforming 'receivers' (the hyperscalers actually writing the checks). That's a much more precise read than the Nasdaq headline number suggests.
items.tweetB: 'Spenders vs receivers' is going to matter more than 'AI winners vs losers' for the next 12 months. The picks-and-shovels side of capex just rallied even while the check-writers wobble on free cash flow.

charts.desc: STD channel charts on SPY, QQQ, IWM, DJIA for the week.

items.title: LatAm currencies have quietly out-returned the dollar — and every major developed-market currency — over 5 years
items.idea: Latin American currencies (ex-Argentina, ex-Chile) have been among the only currencies in the world to outperform the US dollar over five years, beating not just EM peers but virtually every major developed-economy currency too. Costa frames this as the early stages of a new 'LatAm Reawakening' cycle.
items.angle: FX outperformance this broad and this quiet usually leads equity and local-rate re-ratings by quarters — it's the kind of un-crowded macro trade that shows up in currency data long before anyone's positioned for it in equities.
items.tweetA: LatAm currencies have quietly out-returned the dollar AND most developed-market currencies over 5 years — while nobody was positioned for it. FX leads. If that continues, LatAm equities and local rates are the trade still asleep.
items.tweetB: The 'dollar wrecking ball' narrative has dominated for years. Meanwhile LatAm FX — ex-Argentina, ex-Chile — has quietly beaten the dollar for half a decade. Consensus macro narrative and actual price action have been disagreeing this whole time.

charts.desc: 5-year LatAm currency vs USD and DM-currency performance chart (full report paywalled).

items.title: McKinsey's featured chart this week: which human skills get more valuable as automation scales
items.idea: McKinsey's featured chart in this week's digest, 'Human skills in the age of automation,' addresses which human skills become more valuable — not obsolete — as automation spreads through the workforce, alongside companion charts on US import exposure and AI adoption in nursing.
items.angle: Every AI-capex trade prices the compute side of automation; this is the labor-market flip side that determines which jobs actually get displaced versus repriced upward, and it isn't being modeled by anyone trading the AI theme.
items.tweetA: Everyone's trading AI capex. McKinsey's latest is about the other side of the ledger — which human skills actually get more valuable as automation scales, not less. That's the labor-market chart nobody's pricing into anything.
items.tweetB: The AI story in markets is all capex and compute. McKinsey's chart this week asks the question funds don't model: which human skills become scarcer, not obsolete, as automation spreads. Worth opening before the next 'AI takes all jobs' headline.

charts.desc: Featured chart: human skills value vs automation exposure by occupation category.

items.title: McKinsey's other featured chart: US import exposure by sector
items.idea: The same digest features a chart on US import exposure, breaking down which sectors carry the heaviest reliance on imported inputs — the kind of sector-level data that underlies (but rarely accompanies) tariff and reshoring headlines.
items.angle: Tariff and reshoring debates get covered in headlines without ever showing the underlying exposure data by sector — this chart is the part that tells you which industries actually have reshoring optionality and which are structurally stuck importing.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's 'US import exposure' chart this week is a quiet reminder that tariff and reshoring headlines are only half the story; the sector-level exposure data is the part that tells you who actually has optionality and who's structurally stuck.
items.tweetB: Everyone talks tariffs in headlines. Almost nobody looks at the underlying import-exposure data by sector. That's the chart that tells you which industries can actually reshore versus which just eat the cost.

charts.desc: US import exposure by sector chart.

items.title: McKinsey's third chart: trust, not capability, is the bottleneck for AI adoption in nursing
items.idea: The digest's third featured chart, 'Transparency and trust for AI in nursing,' frames healthcare AI adoption as gated by trust rather than technical capability — a pattern consistent with slower-than-expected AI rollout across regulated verticals.
items.angle: If trust is the binding constraint in nursing, that's the same constraint showing up in every regulated vertical — legal, finance, healthcare — and it means the compute buildout is running well ahead of the actual adoption curve in those markets.
items.tweetA: The AI-in-healthcare adoption story isn't a capability problem anymore, it's a trust problem — McKinsey's new nursing data makes that explicit. Every AI-vertical thesis assuming fast adoption because the tech works is skipping this step.
items.tweetB: If trust, not capability, is the binding constraint on AI adoption in nursing, that's the same constraint showing up in every regulated vertical. The compute buildout is way ahead of the trust curve, and that gap is where AI-healthcare multiples get tested.

charts.desc: Nursing staff survey chart on trust/transparency in AI clinical tools.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Goldman ETF desk
items.title: US ETF industry now averages $325B in daily turnover — leveraged products punch far above their AUM weight
items.idea: Goldman's ETF desk head Chris Lucas notes the industry is averaging roughly $325 billion in daily turnover, with leveraged ETFs driving a meaningful share of that activity. June was the second-heaviest month in Goldman's dataset, with cumulative ETF trading reaching $7 trillion.
items.angle: Leveraged ETFs are a small share of AUM but an outsized share of effective market exposure and turnover — meaning a chunk of daily realized volatility in mega-caps is now a mechanical rebalancing artifact, not purely fundamental flow.
items.tweetA: $325B a day in US ETF turnover, and leveraged products are a small slice of AUM but an outsized slice of that volume. That means a chunk of daily realized vol in mega-caps now comes from options-adjacent ETF rebalancing, not fundamentals.
items.tweetB: Cumulative ETF trading just crossed $7T and June was the second-heaviest month on record. Nobody frames 'ETF boom' as a volatility story, but leveraged-ETF rebalancing is now a structural, daily, price-agnostic flow that shows up in every closing-hour move.

charts.desc: US ETF daily turnover and cumulative trading volume chart (preview only; full note paywalled).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Goldman ETF desk
items.title: A DRAM-themed ETF just overtook EWY — investors want pure memory exposure, not Korea beta
items.idea: Goldman's note highlights DRAM-themed ETF flows overtaking EWY, the broad Korea ETF that's served as the default Samsung/SK Hynix proxy for a decade, as investors increasingly want targeted access to concentrated themes (AI, semis, Korea, mining) rather than broad market exposure. The wrapper is starting to matter as much as the underlying trade.
items.angle: When a thematic wrapper takes share from a country ETF holding the same underlying stocks, that's a direct statement investors want the won/political risk stripped out and just want the memory cycle — worth watching for the same pattern in other 'accidental country proxy' ETFs.
items.tweetA: DRAM ETF flows just overtook EWY — the Korea ETF that's been the default Samsung/SK Hynix proxy for a decade. Investors don't want Korea beta anymore, they want pure memory exposure. That's a direct vote that the memory trade is a semis trade, not a country trade.
items.tweetB: When a thematic ETF wrapper takes share from a broad country ETF holding the same underlying stocks, that's not noise — it's the market saying it wants the currency/political risk stripped out and just wants the cycle. Watch for this pattern to repeat in other 'accidental country proxy' ETFs.

charts.desc: No chart in the free preview; full note is paywalled.

items.title: Contango Silver & Gold's whole model: skip the mill, truck high-grade ore to someone else's plant
items.idea: Contango Silver & Gold (CTGO) is building a mid-tier North American precious-metals producer via Direct Shipping Ore — mining high-grade ore and trucking it to third-party mills instead of building its own mill and tailings facility, cutting capex and permitting time. The Manh Choh JV (30% Contango / 70% Kinross) generated $102M in cash distributions in 2025 on ~60,200 gold-equivalent ounces at $1,616 AISC; 2027 guidance is 75,000-80,000 oz at $1,300-$1,400 AISC.
items.angle: DSO economics only work because gold's margin over AISC is wide enough to absorb trucking and toll-milling costs — that arbitrage itself is a signal of how much room this cycle still has, and it's becoming the default junior-miner playbook because permitting speed now beats owning infrastructure.
items.tweetA: Contango's whole model: skip the mill, skip the tailings facility, just truck high-grade ore to someone else's plant. $102M in cash distributions in 2025 from a 30% JV stake, on AISC of $1,616. That capex arbitrage only works because gold's margin is wide enough to eat trucking costs — which tells you how much room this cycle still has.
items.tweetB: Nobody builds a standalone mill anymore if they can help it. Direct Shipping Ore — mine high grade, truck it, toll-process — is quietly becoming the default junior-miner playbook because permitting speed now matters more than owning the infrastructure. Capital efficiency, not resource size, is the new moat in gold mining.

charts.desc: No charts in email; interview transcript format.

items.title: A drill hole at Lucky Shot hit 0.17m at 972.1 g/t gold — roughly 31 oz/ton
items.idea: Recent drilling at Contango's Lucky Shot project confirmed extremely high-grade but locally narrow veins, including 0.17 meters grading 972.1 g/t gold and 5.30 meters grading 8.89 g/t gold. Management itself flags the real challenge as establishing continuity, mineable widths and predictable dilution, not just producing occasional spectacular assays.
items.angle: 972 g/t is a genuinely extreme number even by high-grade underground standards, but narrow bonanza intercepts are common in vein-hosted deposits and routinely fail to scale — the tell to watch is whether the next batch of holes confirms continuity, not this one assay.
items.tweetA: 0.17 meters at 972.1 g/t gold. That's roughly 31 ounces per ton in a single drill interval. Numbers like that get headlines — the real question, which the company itself admits, is whether the vein has enough continuity and mineable width to matter at scale.
items.tweetB: 972 g/t gold in a drill hole is the kind of number that goes viral and means almost nothing on its own — narrow bonanza intercepts are common in vein-hosted deposits and routinely fail to scale into mineable resources. The tell isn't this assay, it's whether the next 10 holes confirm continuity.

charts.desc: No charts in email; interview transcript format.
